text: Herein, we report on the fabrication of an atomically dispersed Mn on an aryl amino-substituted graphitic carbon nitride (g-C<sub>3</sub>N<sub>4</sub>) catalyst (denoted as Mn/C3N4-SSC or catalytic system derived from seawater). Using only seawater as a reactant source (including solution constituents and ambient dissolved gases), the synthesized catalyst catalyzed the effective production of hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>). Of interest to catalytic applications, the as-prepared catalyst powder showed outstanding catalytic activity of up to 2230 μM H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> after reacting alkaline water for only 7 h and achieved a corresponding H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> concentration of 1800 μM within 7 hours by catalyzing seawater under the same experimental conditions while retaining activity after quick catalyst wash-and-use cycling. Notably, the catalytic generation of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> in seawater occurred via a rare two-electron water oxidation reaction (WOR) pathway at pH 10.8 and 8.3, where both direct and indirect processes (photoexcited holes (h<sup>+</sup>)) were found in which one involves one-step photoassisted 2e<sup>-</sup> WOR via which a photoexcited hole (h<sup>+</sup>) directly catalyzes water (H<sub>2</sub>O) oxidation to form hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) and the other is sequential photoassisted holes catalyzing each of water (H<sub>2</sub>O) and hydroxide (OH<sup>-</sup>) oxidation to form hydrogen peroxide (H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub>) via two <sup>•</sup>OH. Information about material structure as well as catalyst structure at the catalytic sites as well as the atomic level in situ (at time=0 and during the course of H<sub>2</sub>O<sub>2</sub> catalytic production reactions) is provided for the first time and the samples at catalytic active sites at the atomic level have been systematically characterized at multiple length scales utilizing techniques ranging from quantum chemistry calculations through electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR), X-ray absorption near edge structure (XANES) spectroscopy, extended X-ray absorption fine structure (EXAFS), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and with magic-angle spinning solid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy. In addition to the respective strengths provided by individual methodologies, multiscale molecular modeling has also been conducted to elucidate sample structural/compositional information coupled with an understanding of catalytic reaction process dynamics such as catalyst reusability associated with structure and properties in the catalytic system derived from seawater provided by the hybrid model which effectively integrated data derived from both classical reactive molecular dynamics simulations and associated quantum chemistry calculations.
explanation: The text uses dense, uniform scientific jargon and long, template-like sentences typical of AI-generated summaries, suggesting a moderate likelihood of AI authorship. However, the presence of many specific experimental details and advanced characterization techniques keeps it plausible as real scholarly prose, making the assessment ambiguous.
